Our striker scored in the final minute.
We were watching at the bar when they scored.
I had never seen a comeback like that.
By the time I arrived, the game had already started.
You're Brazilian, aren't you? Then you must have seen some football. Did you catch the match last night?
I did. What a nail-biter. I've followed that team for years, and I'd never seen a comeback quite like it.
Same here. We were losing two to nothing, and half the pub had already left. Then the striker came on.
And everything changed. That's the thing about a good rivalry, the whole room was gripped until the final whistle.
So who do you actually support? Be honest, are you a real supporter, or just here for the atmosphere?
Real loyalty, since I was a boy. In Brazil, football isn't just a sport, it's who you are. My father took me to my first game, and I've never stopped going.
Did you catch the match last night?
I've been a fan since I was a kid.
We were losing until the very last minute.
I'd never seen a comeback like that.
It's not just a sport, it's who you are.

Marcelo, it's Isla. I still cannot believe last night. I had waited my whole life to see a final like that, and honestly, I had almost lost hope by half-time. We were losing, the away supporters were singing, and I was ready to go home. Then our striker scored, and everything changed. By the time the winner went in, I had lost my voice completely. My dad used to take me to those games when I was a girl, so it meant even more. Anyway, it was the best night of football I have ever had, and I was glad to share it with someone who gets it. Safe travels, and come back to Edinburgh soon.

Welcome back to the show. Today, why football is never only football. Here is the thing people miss. When someone tells you which team they support, they are telling you where they are from, who raised them, and what they believe in. A club is a story that a whole city has been writing for a hundred years. That is why a comeback can make grown adults cry, and why a rivalry can last for generations. The score fades, but the feeling stays. So the next time a stranger asks if you saw the match, do not just give them the result. Give them the story. Tell them how you were losing, how nobody had given up, and how it felt when the ball finally went in. That is the conversation that actually happens.
